coredata.dc:title: On the intersection of molecular bioelectronics and biosensors: 20 Years of C3B
coredata.dc:creator: Aggas, John R.
Walther, Brandon K.
Abasi, Sara
Kotanen, Christian N.
Karunwi, Olukayode
Wilson, Ann M.
Guiseppi-Elie, Anthony
coredata.dc:description: Formed in 2000 at Virginia Commonwealth University, the Center for Bioelectronics, Biosensors and Biochips (C3B®) has subsequently been located at Clemson University and at Texas A&M University. Established as an industry-university collaborative center of excellence, the C3B has contributed new knowledge and technology in the areas of i) molecular bioelectronics, ii) responsive polymers, iii) multiplexed biosensor systems, and iv) bioelectronic biosensors. Noteworthy contributions in these areas include i) being the first to report direct electron transfer of oxidoreductase enzymes enabled by single walled carbon nanotubes and colloidal clays, ii) the molecular level integration of inherently conductive polymers with bioactive hydrogels using bi-functional monomers such as poly(pyrrole-co-3-pyrrolylbutyrate-conj-aminoethylmethacrylate) [PyBA-conj-AEMA] and 3-(1-ethyl methacryloylate)aniline to yield hetero-ladder electroconductive hydrogels, iii) the development of a multi-analyte physiological status monitoring biochip, and iv) the development of a bioanalytical Wien-bridge oscillator for the fused measurement to lactate and glucose. The present review takes a critical look of these contributions over the past 20 years and offers some perspective on the future of bioelectronics-based biosensors and systems. Particular attention is given to multiplexed biosensor systems and data fusion for rapid decision making.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Prof. Dr. A. G.-E. is founder and scientific director of ABTECH Scientific, Inc., manufacturer of microfabricated biochip devices. He is a Fulbright Specialist sponsored by the US Department of State through World Learning and has been appointed to University of Tucumán, Argentina (UT - 2015) and Wroclaw University of Science and Technology, Poland (WUST – 2019). He has been funded by the Burroughs Wellcome Fund (2019). Support was received from the Consortium of the Center for Bioelectronics, Biosensors, and Biochips (C3B®) and ABTECH Scientific, Inc. The authors acknowledge the support of Texas Engineering Experiment Station (TEES) via a TEES Research Professorship.

The authors acknowledge the support of Texas Engineering Experiment Station (TEES) through a professorship to AG-E; ABTECH Scientific, Inc. for providing access to biochip substrates and the support of the consortium of the C3B. The authors thank Dale Conrad and Chris Akers for their artistic talents in creating figure schematics.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: An overview of key elements in the fabrication of bioelectronic biosensors. Clockwise: i) The use of conductive materials such as inherently conductive polymers (ICPs) (polyanilines, polypyrroles and polythiophenes) and carbon nanomaterials (dots, tubes and sheets). ii) biologically responsive polymers such as synthetic and natural hydrogels; iii) microfabricated contacting electrodes (interdigitated, micro-disc arrays, electrochemical cell-on-a-chip) capable of facilitating electrical interrogation via probing and receiving signals. iv) The incorporation of molecular biorecognition receptors such as (nucleic acids, enzymes, antibodies, antibody fragments, MIPs, cells, and tissue fragments). As an example, the deposition via electropolymerization of pyrrole within a bioresponsive hydrogel covalently attached to a microfabricated interdigitated microsensor electrode simultaneously entrains physicochemical conjugates of carbon nanotubes and enzymes to yield an impedimetric enzyme biosensor.

(A) The proliferation of published research in the areas of bioelectronics, biosensors, and biochips as surveyed between 1990 and 2019. Biosensors continue to hold overwhelmingly more influence in today's research compared to biochips and bioelectronics due to the growing demand for rapid point-of-concern assays and the evolution of opportunities in theranostics, physiological status monitoring and with the advent of need for smart front-end measurement devices for the internet-of-things (IoT), remote health and at-home monitoring (Noah et al., 2018). There is projected continued interest in biosensors, specifically implantable devices for health and wellness monitoring. There are a few noteworthy inflection points to consider in the graph above for each of the keywords. Interest in biochips, particularly microarrays, appeared to wane but got jumpstarted by some key papers, e.g. Pat Brown (Schena et al., 1995) demonstrated and established key principles for microarraying that spilled over into establishing present day 3-D printers. (B) Similar survey of “bioelectronics and biosensors” and “bioelectronic biosensors” over the same period. Biosensors that are based on bioelectronic constructs are now an emerging area.

Structure and mechanisms of conduction in inherently conductive polymers (ICPs). (A) Structures of some commonly researched conjugated, inherently conductive polymers: polyacetylene, polyaniline, polythiophene, polypyrrole, and poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene. (B) The conductivity range of inherently conductive polymers spans insulating, semiconducting, and metallic conduction regimes based on the extent of “doping” (carrier density) and mobility. (C) The molecular structure of polyacetylene gives insight into conduction in conjugated polymers via delocalized π-electrons. (D) The electronic bands of PPy: (i) undoped, (ii) polaron, (iii) bipolaron, (iv) fully doped. (E) The changes in molecular bonding upon doping of PPy. (C-E adapted from Le et al., 2017).

Charge transport in inherently conductive polymers and conduction levels and conduction mechanisms in inherently conductive polymer composites. (A) Illustration of the role of persistence length of conjugated polymer chains in influencing the apparent bulk conductivity of the semiflexible poly(9,9-di-n-octylfluorenyl-2,7-diyl) (PFO) and the flexible polyalkylthiophenes, poly(3-(2′-ethyl)hexylthiophene) (P3EHT). Reproduced with permission (Martin et al., 2018) (B) A schematic illustration of (B(i)) the variable range hopping model vs. (B(ii)) the tunneling model. Reproduced with permission (Gueye et al., 2020). (C) A schematic illustration of conduction levels arising from variations in the density of conductive inclusions influences interfacial charge transfer (C(i)) variable range hopping among conductive domains (C(ii)) conduction via limited percolation paths and (C(iii)) conduction via higher density percolation paths. Reproduced from Wijeratne et al. (2018).

General concepts associated with direct electron transfer (DET) using carbon nanomaterials. Electron tunneling occurs across potential barriers as the wave function does not disappear within classically forbidden regions, but only changes from a propagating wave to an evanescent one (A). Achieving this practically requires either spatial localization within a critical distance of ~15 Å (B). (C) Highlights the generational electron transfer regimes, with direct electron transfer being realized using a carbon nanotube. Electrochemical characterization of DET on carbon nanotubes and graphene quantum dots are depicted in D and E, respectively. Wiley Online Library © 2020 (Aggas et al.) for C, IOP Publishing © 2002 (Guieseppi-Elie et al.) for D, and Elsevier © 2013 (Razmi and Mohammed-Rezaei) for E, with permission.

(A) Molecular constituents of bioresponsive hydrogels. By varying the mol % of included monomer and pre-polymer constituents, the biotechnical properties of the hydrogel can be precisely engineered for water content and ionic conductivity. Incorporation of conductive polymers (such as polypyrrole) render the hydrogel electroconductive and accommodates a transition from ionic to polaronic conduction. (B) Hydrogel attachment to the electrode. (B(i)) Electrodeposition of p(Py-co-PyBA) onto the electrode provides carboxylic acid groups for the covalent conjugation to the amine group of AEMA which then accommodates UV-crosslinked attachment of acryloyl-based hydrogels to the electrode. (B(ii)) Electrochemical deposition of polypyrrole onto the electrode serve to intercede to promote the attachment of acryloyl-based hydrogels containing MPB to the electrode via π-π interaction. (C) Two generalized synthetic routes to fabrication of electroconductive hydrogels. A reactive monomer solution (which can include both hydrogel constituents and precursors to conductive polymers) can be crosslinked via UV initiated polymerization or drop cast, electrospun, or prepared as microspheres. While electropolymerization routes require an electrode substrate in which to deposit the polymer, chemical oxidative polymerization routes can be performed in-situ via incorporation of an initiating oxidant (such as FeCl3 or peroxy sulfate). (D) Immunohistochemical analysis of femoral nerve after nerve transection. fluorescent photomicrographs at low (D(i) and D(iii)) and high (D(ii) and D(iv)) power showing an elevated expression of BDNF-like immunoreactivity in nerve in response to D((i) and (ii)) 0 and (D(iii) and (iv)) 3 h electrical stimulation. (E(i)) PVA-PPy/RED ionotropic patch. (E(ii)) Photo of the patch on porcine skin. images of the cryo-sectioned porcine skin showing an elevated delivery of drug nanocarriers in (E(iii)) control experiment of passive diffusion and when (E(iv) RED-driven iontophoresis was applied after 1 h (F(i)) Electrospun poly(l-lactic acid)/PAn nanofiber. (F(ii)) Hybrid construct of hydrogel/electroconductive hydrogels developed by microlithography technique. (F(iii)) an image of dragon developed with 3-D printed silk fibroin-conjugated graphene oxide.

(A) The 3 fundamental bioelectronic circuit elements utilized in biosensors: chemiresistors, chemicapacitors, bio-FETs. Chemiresistors respond to stimuli by changes in conductivity, measured by resistivity or impedance; chemicapacitors respond to stimuli by changes in dielectric constant (and in turn capacitance), measured by changes in reactance (impedance); bio-FETs respond to stimuli by alteration of VDS or VG by accumulation of charge, measured via amperometry. (B–C) Incorporation of bioelectronics circuit elements into commonly known electronic circuits can yield higher sensitivity of measurements via active components. For example, utilizing a chemiresistor in an oscillator circuit to output an AC sin wave where the resultant wave amplitude is dependent on the resistance of the chemiresistor; utilizing a chemicapacitor in an active AC filter where the cutoff frequency of a probing signal is dependent on the overall capacitance; utilizing a pH sensitive reference FET and bio-FET in a differential amplifier circuit, where the resultant output is dependent on the difference between the reference and bio-FET signals.

(A) Schematic of a dual-responsive Wien bridge oscillator circuit biosensor system. Solid state resistors replaced with either glucose or lactate bioresponsive hydrogels resulted in simultaneous real time measurement of each analyte via fusion into a single AC wave. Clark Error Grids showing the resultant glucose and lactate. (B) Schematic illustration of the Electrical Cell Stimulation and Recording System (ECSARA) for Cells in Electroculture showing the instrumentation control and data acquisition system (hardware).

(A) Multiplexing of 5 biosensors embedded in a single micro-electrode chip capable of sensing analytes relevant to physiological trauma (glucose, lactate, pO2, K+, and pH). Data from each sensor is measured periodically and fused via machine learning techniques to arrive at a HISS score (0–4) capable of determining the severity of the trauma. (B) An electrochemical cell on a chip (ECC) embedded in a microfluidic flow chamber. The ECC utilizes 2 separate interdigitated working electrodes, a counter electrode, and a reference electrode. Utilizing 2 different biorecognition elements yields a dual-responsive sensor capable of measuring analytes via a 3 electrode electrochemical cell.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Formed in 2000 at Virginia Commonwealth University, the Center for Bioelectronics, Biosensors and Biochips (C3B®) has subsequently been located at Clemson University and at Texas A&M University. Established as an industry-university collaborative center of excellence, the C3B has contributed new knowledge and technology in the areas of i) molecular bioelectronics, ii) responsive polymers, iii) multiplexed biosensor systems, and iv) bioelectronic biosensors. Noteworthy contributions in these areas include i) being the first to report direct electron transfer of oxidoreductase enzymes enabled by single walled carbon nanotubes and colloidal clays, ii) the molecular level integration of inherently conductive polymers with bioactive hydrogels using bi-functional monomers such as poly(pyrrole-co-3-pyrrolylbutyrate-conj-aminoethylmethacrylate) [PyBA-conj-AEMA] and 3-(1-ethyl methacryloylate)aniline to yield hetero-ladder electroconductive hydrogels, iii) the development of a multi-analyte physiological status monitoring biochip, and iv) the development of a bioanalytical Wien-bridge oscillator for the fused measurement to lactate and glucose. The present review takes a critical look of these contributions over the past 20 years and offers some perspective on the future of bioelectronics-based biosensors and systems. Particular attention is given to multiplexed biosensor systems and data fusion for rapid decision making.

ce:section.ce:para: Bioelectronics is the field of merging biological systems with electronic instrumentation, resulting in a multidisciplinary approach towards technological development that integrates materials science, chemistry, biology, and electrical engineering (Yuk et al., 2019; Zhang and Lieber 2016). The realization of the electrical abio-bio interface has resulted in several tangible benefits ranging from sensors and monitoring systems to direct tissue stimulation devices, and as such, considerable effort has been put into engineering connections between electronics and natural systems; it remains today an active and emerging field of research. The Center for Bioelectronics, Biosensors, and Biochips (C3B®) has focused specifically on engineering this interface, from the fundamental chemistries and physics of the interaction to data processing and fusion to provide translational benefit of these technologies. In this review, several fundamentals and examples of this endeavor are described – the scope will include several primers on key concepts when developing a bioelectronic interface. Following this, applications of the theory will be discussed highlighting biosensor and bioelectronic material development, synthesis, characterization, and post-acquisitional processing, with several critical and prospective discussions maintained throughout.

Four distinct areas have been identified as requisites for the development of biosensors and in turn, bioelectronic biosensors (Fig. 1 ). First, the use of conductive materials such as inherently conductive polymers (ICPs) or conductive carbon nanomaterials, each of which can be molecularly engineered to yield various chemical linkages as well as nano-, meso-, and microstructures or uniquely fabricated by electrodeposition directly onto metallic or semiconductor electrode surfaces of functional devices. Second, incorporation of these conductive materials as inclusions into bioresponsive hydrogels through entrapment or covalent linkage provides for enhanced processing (casting, microfabrication, 3-D printing, etc.), unique physical dimensions and a biocompatible interface. Third, deposition of the electroconductive hydrogels (ECHs) onto microelectrodes, such as paired interdigitated electrodes or 3 electrode electrochemical cell-on-a-chip, renders a manner to probe the changing electrical/electrochemical properties of the bioelectronic structure in response to analyte stimuli. Lastly, incorporation of biorecognition elements (such as enzymes) renders the ability to link the electroconductive response to selectively monitor target analytes.

Fig. 2 shows the prevalence of published articles including the keyword “biosensors”, “bioelectronics” or “biochips” from 1995 to 2019. As expected, since the invention of the Clark oxygen electrode in 1962, the field of biosensors has been expanding exponentially, with an ever-increasing amount of papers published on a yearly basis. While the terms “biochips” and “bioelectronics” have not received as much volume in publication numbers, a clear trend can be seen over the past 10 years. Research with the keyword “biochips” may have seen its zenith (2000–2010), however it appears that research with the keyword “bioelectronics” is beginning to increase rapidly. The increase in research featuring “bioelectronics” can be partly attributed to a report published in 2009 by the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST), which signaled the coming importance and opportunities for bioelectronics in various fields (Walker et al., 2009). The development of complex nano-structured bioelectronics sensors beyond traditional semiconductor and abio-bio interface technologies has burgeoned under the concept of “More than Moore”, signaling the importance of device engineering beyond Moore's Law (Karunwi et al., 2013b).

One of the most widely used constructs in bioelectronic engineering are electroactive polymers. A polymeric compound displaying electrical or electrochemical activity via possession of groups that can be oxidized or reduced is termed a redox polymer. Redox activity in polymers can be imparted both via a conductive backbone (as seen with ICPs like polypyrrole, polyaniline and polythiophene), or by doping with an electrochemically active side chain (Gracia and Mecerreyes 2013). Recently, redox active conjugated polymers have been established by utilizing reversible redox active groups such as quinones, imides, carbazoles, organosulfur, triphenyl amine, or ferrocene. These redox active polymers have been fabricated by incorporation of pendant groups by covalent attachment or through crosslinking of groups along a polymer backbone (Sui et al., 2013). Generally, conductive polymers show a large capacitive envelope as an electrochemical signature when measured via voltammetry, while those with redox centers display faradaic signatures indicative of the corresponding redox center, however, these may be mixed or modulated by both intrinsic factors (chemical properties) and extrinsic factors (film thickness, electrolytes, and more) (Gracia and Mecerreyes 2013). Each of the different types of redox polymers generally have different conductivities, charge transfer kinetics, and capacitive properties (Chen et al., 2020; Kim et al., 2017). Redox polymer electron transfers are generally a gradient from being diffusion to kinetically controlled. This can be investigated electrochemically, with a linear dependence on scan rate indicating kinetically limited reactions (and adherent polymer), and a square root dependence indicating diffusion limited electron transfer described via the well-known Randles-Sevcik equation (Casado et al., 2016). Thorough fundamental treatment of conductivity in PEDOT (Nara et al., 2019) and similar systems may be found in the 4th edition of the Handbook of Conducting Polymers (Reynolds et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: General charge transfer in organic polymeric materials
ce:section.ce:para: ICPs such as polyacetylene (PA), polyaniline (PAn), polythiophene (PTh), polypyrrole (PPy), and poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) (PEDOT) have been extensively researched for their intrinsic conductivities (Fig. 3 A) (Brédas et al., 2002). Based on the fabrication technique and amount of doping, the conductivity of conductive polymers can range several orders of magnitude to act as insulators, semiconductors, and metallic conductors (Fig. 3B). Through fabrication advancements over the last several decades, electrically conductive polymers have been synthesized with conductivities up to 105 S cm−1 (Gachnauer and Gilani 2018). The conductivity of ICPs, which possess highly conjugated backbones, arises from a smearing of wave functions classically associated with alternating single (σ) and double (π) bonds, which yields their inherent electrical, electrochemical, and optical properties (Fig. 3C) (Le et al., 2017). In the double bonds, the second electron pair (p-π) is readily delocalized and allows for transport of charges along the polymer backbone and, via interchain hopping, among polymer chains under the influence of an electric field. Transport of these charges has been documented via solitons (zero charge), polarons (±e charge), and bipolarons (±2e charge).

The electronic conductivity of these charge transporters can be visualized by the band diagram. The band diagram of PPy is shown as an example in Fig. 3-D, which exhibits 4 different band structures dependent on the level of doping (Le et al., 2017). The band diagram of undoped (reduced) conjugated polymers resembles that of a semiconductor – there exists a bandgap between the conduction band and the valence band in which excited electrons (from π-bonds) can jump to the conduction band. Oxidation results in removal of 1 (polaron formation) or 2 (bipolaron formation) π-electron(s) from the polymer chain, resulting in a band gap energy decrease corresponding to the binding energy of the polaron/bipolaron (Fig. 3E). Further oxidation results in an overlap of bipolarons, leading to 2 additional bipolaron bands and further decrease in the band gap.

While the charge transport mechanism in electrically conductive polymers is complex, being situationally dependent upon structure and environment, and not fully understood, several theories have been posited to model interchain mobility, the most compelling of which are electron tunneling (ET) and electron variable range hopping (VRH) (Fig. 4 B) (Beljonne et al., 2018).

Several models of hopping mechanisms have been developed, including Efros-Shkolovski VRH, nearest-neighbor VRH, and Mott VHR – each of which have been found suitable in differing length scales and temperatures (Li et al., 2015) (Fig. 4A–C). Mott's variable range hopping theory, originally developed to describe transport in disordered semiconductors, has been attributed as the dominant mechanism of polaronic charge transport in conjugated polymers at physiologically relevant temperatures (300 K). The conductivity (σ = neμe + phμh where n is electron density, p is hole density, e = h is charge of an electron, and μe and μh are the respective carrier mobilities) in 3-dimensional conjugated polymers is governed by the temperature:

Where σ0 is the overall conductivity, T0 is the activation energy, and n is the dimension of conduction (n = 3). In the VRH model, carriers undergo a thermally activated hopping via a probabilistic manner between two localized states in 3 dimensions (Gueye et al., 2019).

The electron tunneling model was introduced in 1980 by Sheng (1980) to better explain the higher metallic-like conductivities found in highly doped conductive polymers. It was found that transport of charges occurs between the larger conductive segments rather than hopping between localized states. The fluctuation induced tunneling model defines conductivity as:

Where σS, T1, and T2 are constants (Gueye et al., 2019). The temperature dependence of conductivity is therefore a key, critical to both understanding and implementing bioelectronic biosensors based on ICPs.

Bioelectronic engineering refers to any interfacial engineering aimed at connecting biology with electrical technology (Yuk et al., 2019). In several cases, this involves developing technologies which are both electrically active as well as biologically compatible, with function depending on the former and tissue interfacing depending on the latter (Guiseppi-Elie 2010). This then leads to several engineering approaches which utilize natural components as the (partial) basis for the electronic circuit interface, in contrast to using solely silicon or metal-based electronics. One such important class of molecules used in several devices and applications are biopolymers. Biopolymers are naturally occurring polymeric constructs and may be comprised of proteins, sugars, nucleotides, or combinations thereof (Ling et al., 2018; Park et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2018). Biopolymers themselves offer simple to very complex macromolecules and may have sophisticated response behaviors, e.g. photoelectronic, while also being biocompatible, biodegradable, and minimally immunogenic. However, additional engineering may be required to address common issues with mechanical integrity (Vieira et al., 2011) or inherent conductivity (Mohiuddin et al., 2017). The processing and engineering of these materials are not always trivial, as several biopolymers require careful selection of parameters so as not to destroy the natural structure or function (for example, a protein biopolymer can be easily denatured). Thus, to retain activity and function, biopolymers may not be able to go through the harsh processing steps typically associated with electronics.

Biopolymers can be incorporated into bioelectronics through a variety of methods, serving several different roles. One such role is that of simply a biologically compatible scaffold. Several examples of this are present throughout literature. One way to employ them is as a (hydro)gel matrix to support another material (Yuk et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2018). As an example, Netto et al. developed a gas sensor for detection of five volatile gases based on gelatin, agar, and sodium alginate biopolymers (Netto et al., 2020), using the biopolymer as a gel solid scaffold for the ionic liquids. Biopolymers may also be employed as solid-state scaffolds which contain dispersions of ICPs and thereby can support cell growth (Abasi et al., 2019b). However, their function and application are not limited to purely physical scaffolding, as biopolymeric nanostructures resulting from different preparation techniques have themselves been shown to alter apparent electrical properties (Aggas et al., 2018d). And, other biopolymers have been shown to possess inherent conductive properties, either natively or via doping (Hafiza and Isa 2014; Mallik et al., 2002; Salleh et al., 2016). Altogether, biopolymers are an important ingredient for engineering the abio-bio interface for bioelectronics as they offer multifaceted benefits from chemical to physical respects while improving biocompatibility.

While several properties of biopolymers make them attractive candidates for biological applications, they are not necessarily conducive enough to support electronic interfacial engineering. Introducing conductive nanomaterials into the polymeric construct can then supplement the original matrix by imparting improved mechanical, photonic, or electronic properties (Bordes et al., 2009; Xie et al., 2013), yielding what is termed as a nano-bio-composite. One of the great strengths of this approach is that it yields tunable properties based on the ingredients used to form the final composite; however, the drawback is that generally when combining discrete materials each with different desirable properties, sacrifices are made in the composite material as it contains partial properties of the ingredients, necessitating detailed investigations and materials characterization. Modifying the electronic properties potentially introduces several degrees of freedom depending on the task based on the wide variety of electrical techniques available. Amperometry is facilitated by electron transfer as eased by the nanomaterial, however, impedimetric and coulombic techniques which in addition to charge transfer also measure capacitance and resistance represent space in which bioelectronic activity can be monitored. This opens a wide vista of engineering based on designing materials with electrical properties modulated by the nanomaterial inclusion. Here, several examples are discussed.

One such application of these principles was realized in Abasi et al., where they reported the development of a chitosan-based nano-bio-composite blended with PAn nanofibers to render it conductive (Abasi et al., 2019b). As the percent composition of PAn increased, they observed a decrease in resistance of the overall composite (increased conduction) but an overall decrease in cell viability – highlighting the tradeoffs present when engineering nano-bio-composites. They showed that while the passive conductive composites supported the growth of neural progenitor cells, it provided a platform for electrical stimulation of cells. Aggas et al. explored the resistive and capacitive characteristics of the same material (Aggas et al., 2018d) where they found that the formulation and casting of the PAn-chitosan constructs changed its 3-D morphology, which heavily influenced its electronic properties, altering the balance between resistive and capacitive contributions.

This type of tunable nano-bio-composite engineering was developed in Aggas et al. using a nanofibered conductive polymer, polyaniline-polyacrylamidomethylpropane sulfonic acid (PAn-PAAMPSA), as a nanomaterial within a 3-D-printable hydrogel bioink (Aggas et al., 2018a). Notably, they reported that the inclusion of the electrically conductive polymer improved the hydrogels bulk conductivity, while in turn the hydrogel polymer backbone served to provide structural integrity to the nanofibers. This work was further expanded upon in a follow-up study by the same group which established printability of the nano-bio-composites and refined the acuity of the system using poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene):polystyrene sulfonate (PEDOT:PSS) nanomaterials within poly(2-hydroxyethyl methacrylate-co-polyethyleneglycol methacrylate) p(HEMA-co-EGMA) hydrogel (Aggas et al., 2020a).

Biohybrid polymer materials consist of both bio-components such as biomolecules, cells, organically derived polymers and inorganic or synthetic components such as polymers or ceramics (Gao and Maruyama 2021). Within the realm of molecular bioelectronics and biosensors, biohybrid polymers in hydrogels are often utilized to induce biocompatibility and reduce biofouling while simultaneously facilitating electronic and ionic conduction. Zwitterionic polymers such as 2-methacryloyloxyethyl phosphoryl choline (MPC) have found use in the preparation of biomimetic electroconductive hydrogels. MPC has been shown to closely mirror the dynamics of cell membranes, facilitating biointegration and reducing biofouling. Furthermore, incorporation of crosslinkable poly(ethylene glycol) mono-methacrylate (PEGMA) into hydrogels demonstrates enhanced protein resistance on hydrogel surfaces (Abraham et al., 2004), although PEG has since been shown to be immunogenic (Shiraishi and Yokoyama 2019).

Of particular importance in engineering of a conductive hydrogel electrode is the ability to facilitate both ionic and electronic conduction and the transition among these two possible modes. In comparison to “dry” electrode materials (gold, platinum, palladium, p-type silicon), hydrogels with micro- or nano-pores can imbibe large amounts of water and ions suitable for conduction. By incorporation of conductive polymer nanomaterials, electroconductive hydrogels can facilitate both ionic and electronic conductivity while maintaining biocompatibility – so called organic mixed ionic-electronic conductors (OMIECs). It has been shown that ionic transport, ionic-electronic coupling, and electron transport are extremely important dictates of the sensitivity of chemical and biological sensors, as the transduction method in sensors can utilize ionic and electronic conduction. For example, an electroconductive hydrogel capable of ionic to electronic conduction deposited on an electrode and submerged within a physiologically relevant conducting electrolyte will be doped via ion injection at the hydrogel/electrolyte interface. The resultant electronic carrier (h+) will be stabilized by a dopant anion and transported toward the metal electrode. At the electrode/hydrogel interface, a charge transfer can occur (Paulsen et al., 2020). The present understanding of the interconnectedness of these mechanisms, pre/post-processing techniques, nano/micro morphology, hetero/homogeneity, and polymer structure was recently summarized (Paulsen et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Direct electron transfer using carbon nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: Engineering the abio-bio interface is of primary concern when developing functional bioelectronics, as utilizing biological materials in an electrical system requires wiring the two entities into a single, communicating circuit. A major interfacial advancement was taken with the development of nanotechnology, allowing for electrical wiring of biological materials at the length scales at which they operate. Guiseppi-Elie et al., in a series of papers between 2002 and 2013, were the first to demonstrate direct electron transfer (DET) between the co-factor flavin adenine dinucleotide (FAD) of glucose oxidase (GOx) (Guiseppi-Elie et al., 2002) and the copper containing pseudoazurin (Guiseppi-Elie et al., 2005) at glassy carbon electrodes that was enabled by SWNT-enzyme conjugates formed by physisorption or ultrasonic physicochemical conjugation (Karunwi and Guiseppi-Elie 2013). SWNTs, given their tremendous mechanical properties (tensile strength ~30 GPa, Young Modulus ~1 TPa) and good electrical properties (resistivity of 10−4 Ω m, maximum current density of 1013 A/m2) and high aspect ratio, are well suited for the fabrication of bioelectronic biosensors (Karunwi et al., 2013). However, colloidal clays (Lei et al., 2002) and metal oxides (Campbell et al., 2014) have also found favor as candidates in the fabrication of Generation-3 bioelectronic biosensors.

There are several advantages of nanobioelectronics in comparison to using macro-scale materials (Zhang and Lieber 2016). The first is that these materials interface at biomolecular length scale, improving electrical connections via spatial positioning as well as limiting large scale interfacial changes which decreases the risk of altering biological function. Second, they have unique chemical properties conducive for electrical wiring. Between the two large classes of nanomaterials: semiconductor (tubes, wires and dots) and carbon-based (tubes, dots and sheets), carbon nanomaterials offer improved biological compatibility over semiconductor constructs. However, even among the carbon nanomaterials, carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and graphene show poor biocompatibility compared to graphene oxide and carbon quantum dots (GQDs and CQDs, respectively) (Bottrill and Green 2011; Chang et al., 2011; Ding et al., 2014; H. Mü;ller et al., 2010; Hauck et al., 2010; Kostarelos 2008; Lam et al., 2006; Nurunnabi et al., 2013; Shiohara et al., 2004; Winnik and Maysinger 2013). This uniquely positions the last group as a nanomaterial of choice, with overall excellent biocompatibility, for bioelectronic biosensors.

Biocompatibility aside, several of the nanomaterials listed can facilitate direct wiring of biologics into electrical circuits. These types of electrical integrations are referred to generationally (Karunwi et al., 2013c; Kotanen et al., 2012b), and are most commonly applied to biosensing, although generalizations are feasible. A Generation I interface implements the abio-bio interface via monitoring or detection of a biological byproduct – ultimately placing a degree of separation between the biological agent and the circuitry. A Generation II interface then uses a mediator to improve the electronic interface. This may take the form of chemical mediators, or mediating matrices which support the biological agent. A Generation III interface is characterized by direct electrical communication, or DET. This is the ideal situation, as any electronic activity of one component (the biological agent, or the electronic system) is then immediately transferred to the other. DET represents the theoretical peak of functional bioelectronic intimacy, as electronic events are mapped 1:1 between the biological component and the circuitry (Fig. 5 C). Here, integration of carbon-based nanomaterials (CNTs and G/CQDs) into bioelectronics will be discussed in detail as several of the chemistries of these carbon compounds interact favorably with biological materials and have played pivotal roles in the field. Furthermore, the focus will be on establishing direct communication between the materials and the biological agents with special focus on biosensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Direct electron transfer in single walled carbon nanotubes
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanotubes are among some of the most investigated tools for interfacing biological compounds with electrical circuitry thanks to their modifiable carbon chemistries and unique electrical properties (Bachtold et al., 2001; Javey et al., 2003; Wu et al., 2004; Yao et al., 1999). Overall, they were one of the premier innovations for developing DET bioelectronics, however, they were not the first interface used to achieve this result. DET involving redox enzymes has been reported as early as 1977 on cytochrome C (Eddowes and Hill 1977; Yeh and Kuwana 1977), however various other enzymes such as heme containing enzymes (Gorton et al., 1999; Shleev et al., 2005c) and, likely the most researched, glucose oxidase (Guiseppi-Elie et al., 2002).

The first reports of DET with redox enzymes using single walled CNTs (SWCNTs) were in 2002 by Guiseppi-Elie et al., (2002) and for Zhao et al., (2002a) GOx, Wang et al. for cytochrome C (Wang et al., 2002), and Zhao et al. for horseradish peroxidase (Zhao et al., 2002b). Hemoglobin followed in 2004 from Cai et al. (Cai and Chen 2004a) and pseudoazurin in 2005 from Guiseppi-Elie (Guiseppi-Elie et al., 2005). Since those initial manuscripts, an entire field of investigation around the direct wiring of redox enzymes to CNTs has evolved (Cai and Chen 2004b; Liu et al., 2005; Shleev et al. 2005a, 2005b, 2005c; Wang and Wang 2004). With such a breadth of reports and applications on this technique, it is more beneficial to discuss the principles of direct wiring using CNTs rather than exhaustively review literature, as several insights have been developed over time since these landmark papers served to establish the field.

Direct wiring of a redox enzyme with an electrical circuit requires spatial intimacy such that the redox active center of the enzyme is within the electron tunneling distance of the electrical component, in this case CNTs (Luong et al., 2017) (Fig. 5A). It follows therefore that achieving efficient electron transfer involving enzymes with exposed active sites – for example, cytochrome C or hydrogenase – is relatively feasible as random adsorption will likely introduce several interfaces within the tunneling distance (~10 Å) of the conductive nanomaterial (Luong et al., 2017; Varfolomeev et al., 1996). Indeed, these enzymes were reported to display DET with metallic electrodes several years prior to the introduction of CNTs to the field. Another possible mechanism is potential wiring of the redox active center of an enzyme to the protein surface via heme groups. In this approach, the heme group functions as an electrical interconnect; DET without this chemical moiety is often difficult. This scenario is the case for several dehydrogenase enzymes (Bollella et al., 2018). And indeed, adsorption of proteins with these properties onto CNTs is generally successful (Fig. 5B).

For other enzymes where the co-factor is buried more deeply, such as for GOx, the task at hand to obtain DET is significantly more difficult. This is where the beneficial properties of CNTs come into play. In the manuscript describing DET with GOx by Guiseppi-Elie et al., SWCNTs penetrated the enzyme to sit within the tunneling region and facilitate DET (Guiseppi-Elie et al., 2002) (Fig. 5D). The use of SWCNTs is important over their multi-walled counterparts as the former interaction occurs without appreciable denaturation of the protein, compromise of the active site and loss of activity. The diameter of SWCNTs (~1 nm) is on the order of the size of the entrance to the active site (Ma et al., 2010), making interfacing, while difficult, possible (Luong et al., 2017). Notably, this excludes their multi-walled (MWCNTs) counterparts from native DET due to the size disparity and their potential for denaturation of the enzyme. Since the publication of this original work, numerous studies have followed, reporting and applying GOx DET for various engineering endeavors. However, recently, the quality and presence of DET using GOx on CNTs has been critically discussed and questioned based on studies that reproduce characteristic electrochemistries using either adsorbed prosthetic groups/active sites or the presence of a first generational interface (Bartlett and Al-Lolage 2018; Goran et al., 2015; Luong et al., 2017; Wang and Yao 2012; Wooten et al., 2014). These critiques are not unfounded, and moving forward, studying DET on CNTs should be conducted with considerable care and rigor. Several considerations for researchers are worth mentioning. First, it is unlikely that native DET with glucose oxidase (or any other enzyme with a similarly hidden structure) will occur on MWCNTs without additionally wiring the active site to the electrode. Second, several recommendations from Bartlett et al. are worth considering which should be employed for further DET characterization of enzymes on CNTs (Bartlett and Al-Lolage 2018). Purification to remove side reactions is essential, concentration dependence via standard additions should be demonstrated, and controls using compounds other than the substrate are important to isolate non-enzymatic chemistries (Fig. 5C). Alternatively, inhibitor studies may also be employed to similar effect. These considerations should help guide scientists seeking to directly wire redox enzymes into bioelectronic devices and systems in order to ensure function and more importantly, reproducibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Direct electron transfer in graphene quantum dots and carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The more recently discovered graphene and carbon quantum dots are the newest nanotechnology available for nanobioelectronics that have impacted the field significantly. These materials are, in essence, carbon-based quantum dots that may take the form of small pieces of (stacked) graphene or amorphous carbon that are both electrically and photonically active (Du and Guo 2016; Li et al., 2019; Shen et al., 2012; Suvarnaphaet and Pechprasarn 2017; Walther et al., 2020; Zheng et al., 2015). This is profoundly important for bioelectronic engineering as GQDs and CQDs open access to employed photoelectrochemical or electrochemiluminescent methods on top of the electrical interfaces they facilitate, much like CNTs or graphene. Furthermore, these materials are water-soluble due to their surface chemical groups bearing charge – a significant advantage as much biological chemistry is aqueous (Walther et al., 2020). Additionally, GQDs and CQDs are often highly biocompatible (Nurunnabi et al., 2013). These are all attractive qualities for bioelectronic integration. There are some drawbacks, however. Their technical properties appear highly dependent on the method of synthesis (Zheng et al., 2015), and furthermore the fundamental theory of their luminescent and electrical properties are considerably more complicated than that of graphene and CNTs. Several studies have explored the origins of their properties, with the majority weighting to GQDs due to their crystallinity (Mandal et al., 2012; Mueller et al., 2010; Potasz et al., 2012; Ritter and Lyding 2009; Yan et al., 2018). CQDs are amorphous and do not lend themselves as easily to such endeavors, however, progress has also been made focusing on CQDs as distinct from GQDs (Cadranel et al. 2018, 2019; Margraf et al., 2015; Strauss et al., 2014).

These materials furthermore facilitate DET in a mechanism similar to CNTs, with several studies reporting DET with a variety of redox enzymes (Fig. 5E) (see Walther et al. for a comprehensive compilation and review (Walther et al., 2020)). Unlike CNTs that carry substantial theory on the topic, much of the literature on GQDs and CQDs still remains empirical in nature, with the pattern of creating a chemical cocktail that achieves or implements the desired effect. Not surprisingly, this is due to the still vague origins of the physicochemical properties and high sensitivity to surface functional groups and topography – as is revealed by the physics of these nanomaterials. Nonetheless, several of the principles of establishing DET using CNTs still are applicable to GQDs and CQDs, as the most fundamental interaction of electron transfer is preserved between these systems. These materials, despite these current hurdles, offer exceptional promise for interface engineering thanks to several of their positive attributes, as is evidenced by the large bodies of engineering work published within 10 years of their discovery.

A bioresponsive hydrogel is a highly engineered polymer matrix that may contain synthetic and organic moieties capable of interfacing with a natural biological environment in order to elicit a measurable chemical, physical, or biological response. The importance of bioresponsive hydrogels in the realm of bioengineering is underscored by their ever-increasing application in drug delivery devices, tissue and regenerative engineering, sensing and actuating systems, and implantable devices. Their diversity of use is secondary to their ability to respond to external stimuli while concomitantly maintaining a bio-friendly and chemically-modifiable nature. Organic responsive elements are typically incorporated through direct modification of a polymer backbone or sidechain and can consist of polysaccharides, proteins, enzymes, aptamers, and DNA that come directly into contact with target analytes or environments. Alternatively, organic elements can simply be incorporated within the intrapolymer matrix either pre- or post-fabrication. Synthetic responsive elements consist of polymers that can provide improved structural, mechanical, redox, and electrical advantages over organic elements. Polymer selection can be made in order to purposefully manipulate variables such as degree of hydration and water state distribution, elastic modulus, porosity and pore size distribution, oxidative state, and conductivity. Augmenting or synergizing synthetic with organic elements yields potential for an infinite number of novel and exciting bioengineering constructs. The art of engineering a polymer to sense/react to a stimulus while being compatible with crucial performance conditions is challenging but appealing, nevertheless.

Environmentally or stimuli responsive hydrogels (Wilson and Guiseppi-Elie 2013) have found ample use within biosensors for their ability to exhibit both reversible and irreversible physiochemical changes in response to chemical, physical, or biological changes in the environment (Aggas and Guiseppi-Elie 2020). Responsive groups of polymers have been engineered as side chains, chain end groups, and junctions between blocks and can undergo changes in hydrophobicity, conformation, solubility, degradation, and bond cleavage (Cabane et al., 2012). External stimuli have been classified into chemical, physical, or biological depending on the nature of their stimuli.

Chemical stimuli such as ionic strength, pH, or oxidation/reduction reactions have been found to alter interactions between either the polymer/surrounding environment or among the polymer itself. At varying pH, acids and bases are converted between neutral and cationic/anionic states, which lead to changes in polymer swelling and conformation. This concept has been utilized in a pH sensor using responsive hydrogels whereby a combination of hydrophilic/hydrophobic moieties in the backbone of a poly(HEMA-co-PEGMA) hydrogel render it pH-responsive through changing the membrane resistance recorded by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) (Bhat et al., 2020a).

Physical stimuli such as temperature, light, pressure, mechanical stress and electrical/magnetic fields permute the energy levels, microstructure or architecture within the responsive polymer system to elicit a response (van Heeswijk et al., 2019). Thermo-responsive hydrogels change phase, from initially a liquid or solution to a gel in response to a change in temperature which can be accompanied by a large change in electrical impedance (Wang et al., 2019). This can be exploited for bioelectronic biosensor applications: since the gelling point can be engineered to occur at body temperature 37 °C, the hydrogel precursor can be injected into the area of interest with complex geometry and form a gel in-situ, making it an appealing option for bioelectronics. These hydrogels have been reviewed in depth (Klouda and Mikos 2008) (Zhang et al., 2019). Gawade et al. built on this concept by designing a responsive hydrogel where the release of tethered biomolecule can be controlled by a set of stimuli acting in a logic-based controlled-manner (Gawade et al., 2019).

Biological stimuli arising from enzyme-substrate, antigen-antibody, or nucleic acid complement binding may be engineered into responsive hydrogels to indirectly induce responses from their corresponding physiological reactions. As an example, polymers synthesized with boronic acid groups are able to reversibly bind to analytes such as glucose, rendering them extremely useful for biosensing applications. These reversible binding events induce changes in the physicochemical properties, including solubility and overall electrical impedance. Developing hydrogels with specific peptides as crosslinkers or as a tether to immobilize growth factors/drugs to the hydrogel backbone have resulted in enzymatically responsive hydrogels (Aimetti et al., 2009; Purcell et al., 2014). Tethered biomolecules can be released in a controlled manner by cleavage of peptide links by environmental enzymes, as demonstrated by Wilson et al., (2012). Diffusivity of loaded molecules within a hydrogel can increase or decrease as a function of peptide linking or enzyme concentration (Wilson et al., 2012).

Electroconductive hydrogels (ECHs) encompass a large group of soft, swellable polymers with ionic and/or electronic conductivity as illustrated in Fig. 6 A. They have been developed from natural/synthetic polyanion/cations, with inclusion of conductive elements (e.g. carbon, silver, conductive polymers) into a soft hydrogel backbone with organic conductive inclusions. The biocompatibility and biologically-benign characteristics of hydrogels combined with tunable mechanical properties matched to the native tissue modulus and their electrical properties turn this class of material into one of the most widely investigated biomaterials; being utilized as sensor coatings, cell scaffolds, and biosensor transducers (Guiseppi-Elie 2010). Conductive polymer-based ECHs gained popularity as they respond to different types of stimuli (physical, chemical, and biological) and additionally can be functionalized to conjugate to biomolecules, conferring specificity toward target analytes (Aggas and Guiseppi-Elie 2020). The response could reflect changes in the redox state of the polymer, affecting its optical and electrical characteristics. Therefore, several recognition paths have been used: electrochemical-based, optical-based or a combination (Tria et al., 2016).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electroconductive hydrogels for the bio-abio interface
ce:section.ce:para: Several of the ideal properties of ECHs lend themselves to developing the biotic-abiotic interface for bioelectronics. The juncture of foreign bodies (in this case, the electrode) with tissues or cells has been an area of concern evoking research in engineering abio-bio interfaces with the aim of minimizing foreign body responses and maximizing the shelf life and functionality of the abiotic component. One approach to tackle this issue, using carbon-based materials with high biocompatibility, has been discussed in Section 3. In addition to carbon-based materials, ICPs demonstrated great potential as reported by Bhat et al. whereby electroconductive polymer and a biocompatible hydrogel were employed to coat gold electrodes, achieving both biocompatibility and lower charge transfer resistance at the electrode (abio)-neural (bio) interface (Bhat et al., 2020b). In addition to serving as the conductive inclusion, CP is used as a mean to attach the hydrogel to the electrode ensuring the system stability. This is achieved through co-polymerization of new synthesized functional conductive monomers, e.g. pyrrole (Py)-4-(3′-pyrrolyl)butyric acid (PyBA) or PyBA-conj-AEMA with the electrodeposited conductive PPy layer as shown in Fig. 6B(i) and used by Bhat et al. (2020b) or through π-π stacking of the aforementioned functional conductive monomers and electrodeposited PPy film as shown in Fig. 6B(ii) and used by Kotanen et al. (2013b). In this study a bifunctional molecule, 2-methacryloyloxyethyl-4(3-pyrrolyl)butanate (MPB), was synthesized by conjugation of PyBA (acid) and AEMA (amine) which enabled attachment of acryl-based hydrogel to the PPy grown on the electrode through π-π stacking.

ICPs have been widely used in developing biotransducers. ICP-based biosensors utilize the change in the conductive state of the conductive polymer as a result of a chemical reaction or the optical properties of the polymer as the signal. ICPs also provide a means to entrap biorecognition receptors, e.g. glucose oxidase (GOx), as reported by Kotanen et al., who used the in-situ electropolymerization to entrap (or bio-immobilize) the GOx into the hydrogel as conceptually demonstrated in Fig. 6C. The reaction of glucose and GOx provides electron(s) to change the redox state of the PAn which subsequently is detected as an increase of current in a chronoamperometry measurement. The sensitivity of the resultant biosensor depended on the electropolymerization charge density, which was highest at 1 mC/cm2 (Kotanen et al., 2013a) (Kotanen and Guiseppi-Elie 2012). The crosslinking density of the coated hydrogel is likewise important in the response of the transducer as high crosslinking density leads to lower diffusivity of hydrogen peroxide, the byproduct of glucose reaction catalyzed by GOx, effacing the biosensor's performance (Kotanen et al., 2013a) (Kotanen et al., 2012a). Milakin et al. increased the sensitivity and limit of detection of ascorbic acid by adsorbing PAn formed by oxidative polymerization onto a screen-printed carbon electrode. The lower detection limit of PAn-based sensor was attributed to the formation of aniline dimer-based entities formed during synthesis in dilute acidic and neutral pH conditions which have higher redox potential, ERedox ≈ 0.45 V, and hence were easier to reduce. No interfering response from lactate and citrate was observed (Milakin et al., 2013).

The charge density that was used to electropolymerize the conductive polymer layer that served to link the hydrogel layer to the electrode, directly affects the performance of the system whether in terms of reduction of charge transfer resistance of the interface (Bhat et al., 2020b), elastic modules of the resultant system (Kotanen et al., 2013b) or the interaction of living cells with the transducer (Justin and Guiseppi-Elie 2010). Kotanen et al. showed that changing the charge density (1–900 mC/cm2) was concomitant with an increase in elastic modulus from 56 (±32) to 499 (±293) kPa and a decrease in % hydration (68–0%). The membrane electrical resistance of the transducer underwent a decrease from 80 Ω at charge density of 0 mC/cm2 to 69.3 (±0.3) Ω at 900 mC/cm2 and a transition from ionic to polaronic conduction.

Delivery vehicles for electrostimulated release of any molecules/ions is another potential application of hydrogels in general which can be augmented with higher levels of control and specificity in ECHs. ECH-based on ICPs extends the opportunity of developing switchable electro-actuated release by driving the conductive polymer through its oxidized/reduced forms. Hodgson et al. developed a supramolecular composite of PPy and showed the trapped enzyme, horseradish peroxidase (HRP), could be released by reduction and oxidation of the PPy. The enzyme remained active within the hydrogel and the release was higher during reduction, likely due to the cationic nature of HPR (Hodgson et al., 1994). Guiseppi-Elie et al. used the changes of redox state of PAn for ion release under electrical stimulation and showed how augmenting the delivery system with an interpenetrating network of hydrophilic hydrogel led to a higher level of electroactivity and accelerated cation transport by decreasing ion diffusivity within the ECH. The difunctional 3-sulfopropylmethacrylate (SPM) monomer, that was used created an ionic link between the negatively charged hydrophilic hydrogel network and the positively charged PAn, and allowed electro-stimulated release of trapped ions within the gel at lower voltages (Guiseppi-Elie et al., 1998). These studies paved the way for developing more complex ECHs for targeted drug delivery, referred to as bio-smart hydrogels. The changes in the conductivity and optical properties of PPy was used to sense chemical species, e.g. hydrazine (N2H4) and Dithiothreitol (DTT), which are capable of reducing the conductive polymer. The electro-release was based on the fact that when an anionic drug is incorporated in the electropolymerized PPy, the drug stays electrostatically attached to the positively charged PPy. However, if the PPy was driven to electrochemically neutral, the incorporated charged drug was released from the membrane. Reynolds et al. showed the release of adenosine triphosphate (ATP) from PPy/ATP membranes by applying a potential of −0.7 V to the membrane (Pernaut and Reynolds 2000). Brahim et al. developed an amperometric ECH-based biosensor by physically entrapping enzyme into poly(HEMA)/PPy hydrogel for detection of glucose, cholesterol, and galactose. The biosensor was enhanced by copolymerization of dimethylaminoethyl methacrylate (DMA) monomer with HEMA, yielding a pH responsive network to allow a pH-dependent change in the ECH swelling degree. They augmented the passive biosensor to a sense-act system by loading the ECH with both GOx and insulin. The production of gluconic acid, a byproduct of glucose and GOx reaction, enabled of protonation of the amine group of DMA resulting to the network expansion. This mechanism was used to effect and control insulin release, in response to the glucose concentration. This glucose-stimulated controlled release insulin system has the potential of being used as an artificial pancreas (Brahim et al., 2002). Abidian et al. applied electric voltage to release bioactive species from PEDOT coated PLLA-PLGA nanofibers preloaded with the drug. The dexamethasone-incorporated PLLA-PLGA nanofibers were collected on an electrode onto which PEDOT was then electrodeposited. While the charged PEDOT coating normally restricted the release of the preloaded dexamethasone, under an applied positive voltage and in order to maintain charge neutrality a hydrodynamic pressure inside the nanotubes caused the drug to be released, extending a controlled release system (Abidian et al., 2006). An et al. used a PPy-incorporated poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA-PPy) ECH to transfer the electrical current from a reverse electrodialysis (RED) battery for transdermal delivery of charged therapeutics through iontophoresis shown in Fig. 6E(i) and (ii). The RED battery developed from cation and anion exchange membranes and operated by transport of Na+ and Cl− ions between cathode and anode. The current provided by the RED battery then transferred to the PVA layer loaded with negatively and positively charged drug nanocarriers through PPy layer to facilitate/accelerate the penetration of drugs across the skin in a non-invasive fashion compared to a passive diffusion delivery as shown in Fig. 6E(iii) and (iv) (An et al., 2020). As the hydrogel could be engineered to be responsive to several stimuli, dual-/multi-responsive drug delivery could be achieved. Ge et al. suggested PLGA-PEG-PLGA incorporated PPy nanoparticles for a thermos- and electro-actuated release of drug (Ge et al., 2012) and Qu et al. developed a copolymer of chitosan-PAn crosslinked with oxidized dextran for pH and electro-actuated release (Qu et al., 2018). Such level of control has paved the way to localized on-demand and minimally invasive electro-stimulated drug delivery which is highly desired in wound healing, regenerative medicine and transplant medicine.

The biomimetic properties of the soft, highly swellable hydrogels and inherently conductive properties of the electroactive inclusion, results in an electroconductive composites with likely prospect for a scaffold in tissue and regenerative engineering. As has been discussed in several reviews, the high water content, tunable mechanical properties, inclusion of micro and macro pores, functionalizable, neutral or charged, adjustable hydrophilic and amphiphilic properties has allowed this class of material to meet the criteria for an ideal cell scaffold (Lu et al., 2019a). The inclusion of conductive materials adds yet another level of complexity and controllability to the ECH. Primarily suggested for electroresponsive cells, ECH scaffolds showed great potential for variety of cells/application including but not limited to neural cells (Bieberich and Guiseppi-Elie 2004), myoblast (Guo et al., 2019), cardiac patch (Talebi et al., 2020), nerve conduit (Abidian et al., 2012), and wound dressing (Talikowska et al., 2019). ECH often provide functional groups and/or allows functionalization in order to anchor biomolecules such as growth factors or hormones which their presence are crucial in regulating cellular responses the scaffold (Gomez and Schmidt 2007). Processing of the hydrogel/composite resulting in inclusion of pores is another tool available to regulate cellular response, as was shown by Abasi et al. Their study reaffirms the importance of taking cell specific requirements as the two cell lines studied, fibroblast and neural progenitor, responded quite differently to the electrical and topographical features of the scaffold (Abasi et al., 2019b). Additionally, ECH provides a path to apply electrical stimulation to cells, a treatment that with high potential of regulating cellular responses by affecting the charge distribution across cellular membrane (Fried et al., 2009), orientation and migration (Lu et al., 2019b), and gene expression (Geremia et al., 2007) in cells. In Fig. 6D(i) and (ii), the overexpression of brain derived neurotrophic factor like (BDNF-LI) during the course of femoral nerve repair in response to 0 and 3 h electrical stimulation, respectively are shown (Geremia et al., 2007). ECH not only provide passive cues per se but reduce the resistance of the scaffold toward electrical current (Abasi et al., 2019a), therefore presenting a facilitated movement of charge among cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Emerging fabrication techniques for electroconductive hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: The conductive properties of ICPs endure the capability of developing nano-scale diameter fibers through electrospinning (Frenot and Chronakis 2003). This fabrication technique enables 3-D structures with another level of complexity, fiber direction. It introduces a topographical complexity to developing biomimetic materials in tissue engineering as the nano-scale fibers mimic the conductive fibrous structure of the extracellular matrix (ECM), especially in conductive tissues such as cardiomyocytes (Kai et al., 2011; Wang et al., 2017) (Fig. 6F(i)). In recent years, advances in biofabrication techniques such as 3-D printing, microlithography and soft lithography have been used in conjugation with novel bioinks to create customized constructs capable of addressing the needs of personalized medicine. The tandem combination of UV-based fabrication and extrusion printing offer a large degree of freedom to hydrogel processing. Aggas et al. developed a conductive hydrogel precursor by addition of PAn-PAAMPSA nanomaterials to a cocktail of several monomer and pre-polymer. The hydrogel precursor was crosslinked with UV irradiation through microlithography and/or 3-D-prinintg (Fig. 6F(ii)). The inclusion of >0.1 wt% PAn-PAAMPSA was enough to achieve percolation and reduced the membrane resistance by 40% and 14% in microlithography and 3-D-printed constructs, respectively. Resultant hydrogels had no cytotoxicity on NIH/3T3 and PC-12 cells on the collagen coated hydrogels (Aggas et al., 2018a). Ajiteru et al. used a customized digital light processing printer to produce a photocurable hydrogel of graphene oxide and silk fibrin (Ajiteru et al., 2020) (Fig. 6F(iii)). The opportunity and feasibility of personalizing the 3-D printer with expanding approach toward open-source codes and trimming bioinks extend huge promise to this field. Podstawczyk et al. developed a conductive bioink based on magnetic nanoparticles embedded in alginate-methylcellulose. Magnetic nanoparticles not only endowed magnetic properties to the ink but alter the physiochemical properties of the ink, serving as a nanofiller. The ink produced 3-D-printed magneto-hydrogel with complex structure and high flexibility (Podstawczyk et al., 2020). Employing CP, whether to enable complex processing such as electrospinning or improve the mechanical properties of the hydrogel precursor to allow novel fabrication techniques, 3-D printing, has augmented the use of these materials in bioelectronics applications.

Resistive and capacitive properties of polymers have rapidly become the basis for biosensing technology with patents for selective chemically sensitive devices starting as early as the 1960s and quickly accelerating through the 1970s (Guiseppi-Elie et al., 2006). Chemielectronic responsiveness of a polymer describes the selective changes to the measurable resistance (chemiresistor) or capacitance (chemicapacitor) of the construct in response to biological or biochemical reactions in the surrounding micro- and nano-environment. Practically speaking, chemielectronic responsive polymers have found use in bioelectronics as transducers, size-exclusion elements, ion-exclusion elements, and linkers and mediators to biorecognition molecules. Their design and implementation have evolved considerably since their inception, but all began with the basis of electrical device engineering principles that now serve as the foundation for biosmart polymers and the bioelectronic technologies within which they are integrated.

Resistors, which are designed to potentiate an electronic current, and capacitors, which are designed to store electrical energy, are two of the most basic components used in electronic circuits. Commonly engineered resistive components remain at static values (i.e. resistance is not intended to change) while others have been designed to respond to external stimuli. For example, the thermistor (or “thermal resistor”) which was first reported by Michael Faraday in 1833 exhibited variable resistive behavior dependent on temperature. In its simplest form, a thermo-responsive material is placed between two electrodes, and changes in electrical resistance is measured with an ohmmeter as a function of temperature. Building on the concepts of Faraday, chemiresistors were later constructed to exhibit altered electrical resistance in the presence of target analytes. Materials, including metal-oxide semiconductors, CNTs, hydrogels, graphene, conductive polymers, and their hybrid systems have been utilized in chemiresistors for biomedical applications (Kwon et al., 2020).

In practical applications, chemiresistors consisting of a biosensitive material are fabricated on parallel plate or interdigitated co-planar electrode arrays and serve as the interceding layer between the two electrodes (Fig. 7 A). Given fixed electrode geometry, changes to chemiresistor impedance are the direct result of changes in the surrounding biochemical environment that arise from target analytes. Chemiresistors in biosensors typically operate in conductometric mode, whereby measurable changes in electrical conductivity of the transducer are secondary to chemical, and therefore conductive, changes of the polymer and may be the result of redox reactions, protonation/deprotonation, or doping/dedoping (Alam et al., 2018). Regardless of the recognition mechanism, the apparent change in resistance of an enzyme chemiresistor can be approximated by a modified form of the Hill equation, which has been found to appropriately model approximation of several forms of biosensors (Banica 2012; Kurganov et al., 2001):
(3)
Δ R =
R lim

R 0

R

(
c K
)
h
Where ΔR is the change in resistance of the chemiresistor in the presence of an analyte (Ω), R
lim is the limiting resistance (Ω), R
0 is the resistance of the chemiresistor in the presence of no analyte (Ω), c is the concentration of analyte (mM), K is the binding constant of the analyte, and h is the Hill coefficient.

Conductive polymers were found to be excellent candidates in chemiresistive bioelectronics systems as they exhibit good electrical conductivity, fast redox switching speeds, operation at room and physiological temperature, ease of electro-polymerization, ease of material processing, and functionalization with biosensing molecules through chemical/physical attachment (Fratoddi et al., 2015). When typically formed, CPs such as PAn, PEDOT, or PPy sustain a positive state, which renders the intrinsic charge carrying ability on the backbone of the polymer. The incorporation of counter-anion dopants are used to remove or add electrons from the polymer chain, which renders delocalized charges (Park et al., 2013). Thus, the usefulness of conductive polymers such as PAn in chemiresistors stems from its ability to switch between conducting and insulating states through the amount of doping or oxidation.

Chemiresistor devices can be deposited to electrode substrates with relative ease through traditional biofabrication techniques such as drop-cast deposition (Aggas et al., 2018b), dip coating (Guiseppi-Elie 1994), spin-coating, layer-by-layer assembly, spray coating, electropolymerization (Mousa et al., 2019), micro-lithography (Aggas et al., 2018a), 3-D printing (Podstawczyk et al., 2020), or a combination of techniques. The pH sensitivity of CPs such as PAn and PPy have found use in conductometric and potentiometric biosensors, where the local concentration of H+ ions can affect the degree of polymer protonation. In contact with strong acids, the conductivity of the chemiresistor can vary by orders of magnitude, which can be probed through AC or DC signals (Guiseppi-Elie 1994).

Novel chemiresistors fabricated from conductive polymers incorporated into hydrogels rendered bioactive with enzymes such as GOx and LOx have found use in several types of biosensors, including amperometric, voltametric, conductometric, and impedimetric by exploiting both the AC and DC characteristics of the transducer. Kotanen et al. reported on a Generation 1 amperometric glucose sensitive chemiresistive systems which comprised 1) electrodeposited PPy with adsorbed or covalently tethered GOx immobilized on its surface, or 2) pyrrole monomer electropolymerized in the presence of GOx and entrained within (Kotanen et al., 2013a). Continued effort led to the development of a Generation 2 amperometric dual-responsive lactate and glucose biosensor fabricated on a platinum micro-disc electrode array (MDEA 5037 Pt). The system utilized layer-by-layer electropolymerization of alternating PPy-PSSA and PPy-Enzyme (GOx or LOx) encased in p(HEMA) based hydrogels (Kotanen et al., 2018). Other studies utilized electropolymerization of aniline and (N-phenyl-o-phenyldiamine) on gold thin film electrodes with subsequent drop-casting of GOx/chitosan yielding high surface area nano-fibers and nano-flakes with excellent glucose sensitivity (Mousa et al., 2019).

Morphology and quantity of conductive polymer inclusion in a chemiresistor system can directly affect the resistance of a system or induce capacitive (leakage) effects. For example, by using PAn-Cl/Chitosan blends at varying wt%, Aggas et al. showed that 1) purely resistive elements of PAn-Cl were easily produced using the drop-cast method, 2) incorporation of chitosan (a commonly used natural biopolymer) could effectively alter resistivity and capacitance, and 3) lyophilization of PAn-Cl/Chitosan composites yielded porous networks of connected pores which led to a large increase in capacitance (Aggas et al., 2018c). This body of work demonstrated the direct impact of polymer composition and morphology in the control of the resistive and capacitive contributions of polymer hybrid systems, purely from a materials engineering standpoint, independent of biological elements.

Chemicapacitors are often found with the same electrode architecture as chemiresistors (parallel plate or interdigitated electrode array) (Fraden 2004). In a chemicapacitor system fabricated in the parallel plate method, the capacitance, C (F), is defined as:
(4)
C =
ε 0
ε
f i l m
A
d
Where ε
0 is the permittivity of free space (8.85 × 10−12 Fm−1), ε
film is the changing dielectric constant of the CP (Fm−1), A is the electrode plate area (m2), and d is the distance between the parallel plates (m). Biochemical interactions with the target analyte and the transducing chemicapacitor result in changing capacitive reactance of the transducer (Khan et al., 2016). Utilizing electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), detailed information of resultant changes in the dielectric constant of chemicapacitor system can be used in real time to discern biological events for continuous biosensing applications (Li 2008; Yang et al., 2011).

Two major concerns plague chemiresistors and chemicapacitors devices in bioelectronics. Firstly, being real, they are non-ideal, as such are each subject to parasitic effects, resistors to capacitive influences and capacitators to resistive leakage. Secondly, chemiresistive and chemicapacitive responses are non-specific and so are subject to strong non-specific influences from dielectric constant changes of the medium, Donnan partitioning of ions, and temperature effects. Chemiresistive and chemicapacitive bioelectronic biosensors are thus best realized in bridge circuits such as Wheatstone and Kelvin Bridge circuits.

The use of field effect transistors (FET) as a biotransducer component in biosensing modalities has been realized through the development of ion-selective field-effect transistors (ISFET), antibody-based field-effect transistors (Immuno-FET), cell field-effect transistors (CELLFET) and enzyme-based field-effect transistors (ENFET). There are several Bio-FET architectures that are commonly used within biosensing – one such architecture is shown in Fig. 7A.

The ENFET biosensors, which are based on ISFET systems, utilize immobilized enzymes between the source and drain (often in the presence of conductive inclusions such as PPy or CNTs encapsulated within a membrane). The reaction of a substrate with the enzyme results in accumulation of charge carriers between the source and the drain proportional with the concentration of analyte. In this instance, any electrical/chemical changes at the interface of the gate and electrolyte or the gate and the channel are measured as a change in an electrical current: the local charge carrier concentration modulates the effective conductance of the transduction layer, resulting in measurable changes in drain-source current (Nguyen et al., 2019; Vu and Chen 2019). In a given FET utilizing the reaction of hydrogen peroxide on platinum electrodes (such as Gen I biosensors), the changes in gate voltage (VG) are governed by:
