Where k is the Boltzmann constant, T is the temperature, q is the charge of an electron, CG-E is the capacitance at the double layer of the gate and electrolyte, CE-C is the capacitance at the double layer of the electrolyte and channel, and C is a constant.

Incorporation of various soft bioelectronic components into more sophisticated circuitry has resulted in fabrication of novel circuitry and sensing devices including analog RC filters, Wien bridge oscillators, voltage divider networks, Wheatstone bridge networks, and sample and hold circuits. The incorporation of stimuli responsive bioelectronic components into flexible circuits, that have until recently been reserved for solid state electronics, marks an important step toward fully organic electronic circuits capable of indwelling active performance in the human body.

Bioelectronic circuits can be created by utilizing components discussed: chemiresistors, chemicapacitors, and FETs. The use of oscillators in piezoelectric and acoustic biosensors is deeply understood, however the use of electronic oscillators (Hartley, Colpitts, Wien-bridge, etc.) in electrochemical biosensors using chemiresistors/chemicapacitors has only been studied recently (Aggas et al., 2020b; TermehYousefi et al., 2017). One of the simplest forms of an oscillator uses a Wien-bridge: a frequency selective form of a Wheatstone bridge, where 2 RC networks (low-pass and high-pass) render AC oscillations at a resonant frequency (fr). In the Wien-Bridge oscillator shown in Fig. 7B, the gain of the output signal (Av) is governed by the relationship between R1 and R2:
(6)
A v
= 1 +
R 1
R 2

When R1 is replaced with an analyte sensitive chemiresistor, the varying resistance results in an increase/decrease in the AC output signal (Fig. 7C). By utilizing an active component (operational amplifier), even small changes in resistance of the chemiresistor are amplified, yielding higher sensitivity than utilizing a chemiresistor by itself.

Simple low pass RC filter circuits created with CPs have been demonstrated, where the inherent frequency dependent impedance of the CP was utilized to replace the RC network (Liu et al., 2003; Sriprachuabwong et al., 2010). While the impedimetric properties of conductive polymers often represent that of an RC circuit, clever manipulation of a polymer film (composition and morphology) can render it more resistive (frequency independent) or capacitive (frequency dependent). By varying the wt% of conductive polymer, wt% of scaffolding agent, amount of doping, or post-processing such as, solvent annealing, lyophilization or air drying, different impedimetric properties can be emphasized (Abasi et al., 2019b). More robust and selective systems can be implemented by incorporating an operational amplifier and replacing only the R or C with a responsive chemiresistor or chemicapacitor (Fig. 7B). Through continuous application of a low amplitude, low frequency AC signal (with or without a DC excitation), the chemiresistor/chemicapacitor can be effectively probed, and the resultant waveform can be amplified by the operation amplifier for measurement (Aggas et al., 2020b). The cutoff frequency of the filter (fc) is thus a function of both the resistor and capacitor:

By replacing the capacitor with an analyte responsive chemicapacitor, changes in the capacitance result in a frequency shift of the low-pass filter (Fig. 7C).

By pairing a bioresponsive FET electrode (such as an ENFET) with a reference FET (without biorecognition elements), differential circuits can be fabricated to deal with common problems encountered with temperature and drift (Anastasova et al., 2018). In the circuit shown in Fig. 7B, the differential pair of an ENFET and REFET are first amplified, at which point the difference between their signals is rendered through a differential amplifier. In instances where the redox reaction between an enzyme and substrate creates hydrogen peroxide, the oxidation of conductive polymers in the gate or channel of the ENFET will alter the output of the ENFET while keeping a proper reference point via the REFET (Fig. 7C). According to op-amp theory, the output signal (Vo) is the difference between the 2 input signals:

Where the gain (AD) is determined by ratios of specific resistors in the amplifier circuitry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Illustrated examples of bioelectronics in biosensors

ce:section.ce:para: A Wien-oscillator using organic enzyme-chemiresistors capable of continuously measuring glucose and lactate via a single output sine wave has been recently reported (Aggas et al., 2020b) (Fig. 8 A). The system, which utilized a voltage divider DC offset appended to the output of the Wien-bridge oscillator (Section 5.2.1), comprised two enzyme-chemiresistors fabricated from PPy-co-PyBA and conjugated to either GOx or LOx within a photo-crosslinkable p(HEMA)-based hydrogel. The RC network (R1C1, R2C2) was designed to create steady sine wave oscillations at 1.0 Hz, which was found to be low enough to probe charge transfer characteristics of the chemiresistors. The feedback resistor of the oscillator, which dictated the amplitude of the resultant 1.0 Hz wave was replaced with a glucose-sensitive chemiresistor, and a resistor in the voltage divider network was replaced with a lactate-sensitive chemiresistor. The resultant output wave would decrease in AC amplitude upon interrogation in higher glucose concentrations and increase in DC offset upon interrogation in higher lactate concentrations. The entire bioelectronic system was built on an Arduino Uno R3 which transmitted all data to a computer station via Bluetooth®, where the AC amplitude and DC offset were deconvoluted and used to calculate the concentration of each analyte via the modified Hill equation (Eq. (3)).

By using a complex circuit such as an oscillator, there are a number of possible methods for data fusion at the signal level. That is, with an AC signal output where the frequency, amplitude, phase, and DC offset are “functions” of individual components in the circuit, a single oscillator system can incorporate several biosensing elements that all share the same output signal. Therefore, the output a single oscillator circuit can carry and convolute information originating from a multitude of biotransducers which can subsequently be deconvoluted by computation or used as an input into another system (such as the closed-loop feedback systems used in implantable continuous glucose monitor/insulin pumps).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrical cell stimulation and recording apparatus (ECSARA)
ce:section.ce:para: Abasi et al. developed a dual-function electrical cell stimulation and recording apparatus (ECSARA) that allowed for simultaneous application of a uniform electric field to cells seeded on a cell culture insert as well as real-time monitoring of cellular behavior via simultaneous recording of Trans Endothelial/Epithelial Electrical Resistance from Multiplexed Multi-frequency Bioelectrical Impedance Spectroscopy (M-MBIS) of the cells (Fig. 8B) (Abasi et al., 2020). The system comprised a 24-well electroculture ware having the same footprint as a common 24-well cell culture plate with 24 independently addressable rod titanium electrodes embedded in the lid and a single planar electrode with isolated regions at the bottom of each well. The electrical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and subsequent equivalent circuit analysis (EQCA) were used to interpret the impedimetric response of cells as they came to confluence and established tight junctions. The model used by EQCA was based on modified Randles model with additional QR blocks according to the complexity of the measurement. The electrode material was chosen from a range of options (titanium, graphitic carbon, etc.) depending on the application. Graphitic carbon electrodes were found to possess a low charge transfer resistance (RCT) without any specific requirements for polishing in contrast to a large RCT measured for titanium electrodes (due to growth of an oxide layer) (Abasi et al., 2020). However, the large RCT leads to delivery of capacitive charge to cells in the stimulation mode of ECSARA which is speculated to be safer for cells. The control of the device and synchronization between stimulation/recording modes was automated via a custom-developed code in LabVIEW. A five-day stability study with titanium electrodes and PBS electrolyte showed a stable impedance recording with no significant fluctuation in the solution resistance (RS) over time. At the same time, the variability among wells was shown to be at an acceptable level, the solution resistance (PBS) of 23 wells was 37 ± 5 Ω cm2 with only one well showing any significant statistical difference. The adsorption of serum protein onto electrodes did not significantly affect the impedance. ECSARA was used to monitor the growing confluence of HUVECs. The EIS data collected from the cells cultured on inserts were modelled using an RS(QCELLRCELL)(QOXROX)(QDLRCT) equivalent circuit where the changes in QCELL and RCELL representing the transmembrane epithelial/endothelial cell resistance (TEER) and capacitance of the cells, respectively were monitored. A 1.5-fold increase in TEER resistance concurrent with a 2.3 fold increase in QCELL were reordered as the cells grew to confluence and confirmed by VE-cadherin expression.

These systems have the potential to greatly augment science and healthcare by parallelizing biosensors into multiplexed systems capable of simultaneously monitoring several different analytes. Multiplexed systems can be envisioned which take in large amounts of data in real-time and then process these data to yield new information. Approaching this is a difficult technical problem, however. First, systems need to be sufficiently parallelizable, as cross-reactions or interference renders outputs generally untrustworthy. Second, a process for processing large amounts of data in rationalized ways must be at hand. Thirdly, the time cycle of multiplexed measurements must be compatible with the update changes in analyte concentrations. Finally, the information processing must itself be verified and analyzed for actionable information. The power of such combinations though may lead to several sophisticated, intelligent systems which may facilitate several important processes from healthcare monitoring and diagnosis to developing engineered interacting biological systems for drug discovery. In time, as the specifics of individual systems improve in understanding, applying them together creates a new sphere of investigation. Two examples of multiplexed systems are shown in Fig. 9 A and B. In the first system, one can envision several different sensing regimes on an electrode device each chemically modified to detect medically relevant analytes. This device can then be implanted for real-time monitoring of a patient's physiological status and produce actionable scores for physicians. The information fusion of these parameters is explored in Bhat et al., (2020c). The second system is a polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) microfluidic device with an electrode system placed in the flow field. Thanks to the interdigitated arrays, each side may be individually functionalized to detect a specific analyte and measure concentrations electrochemically. Feasible applications of these conceptual technology see applicability in medical or diagnostic labs, flowing whole patient blood over the electrode to detect specific blood-borne analytes (for example, pathogens). However, this fluidic detection regime is not limited to healthcare, and functions similarly for several aqueous systems provided the sensor can be engineered.

Future applications of these technologies have the capacity to revolutionize healthcare in how physicians acquire and process data for decision making. Multiplexed biosensing or monitoring systems like those discussed here thus empower physicians with large sets of data provided in real time and serve as tangible translation value for these technologies. However, big data requires processing and fusion for actionable value to be provided to the physician to optimize their time. Post-processing and data fusion algorithms are currently an active area of research, exploring ways to organize, validate and present derivative data derived from such biosensing systems (Bhat et al., 2020c; Durrant-Whyte and Henderson 2016; Habib et al., 2016; Lee et al., 2008; Liggins II et al., 2017; Meng et al., 2020; Muzammal et al., 2020; Uddin et al., 2020). The overall goal of such investigations is to “make sense” of the large amounts of data provided by bioelectronic systems and yield actionable data which informs physician decision making. Ideally, these techniques give tools for the healthcare field which optimize their time towards patient care and minimizes time spent triaging, finding, or otherwise searching out patients who require stratified level of care. Other potential benefits lie in pattern recognition while sifting through medical data, giving the physician a holistic view of the patient's status.

Realizing these benefits, however, is difficult, and there is no one correct way to perform data fusion. Several approaches may rely on simply imposing of prior knowledge onto data sets to develop a first order filter. These efforts are bolstered by machine learning and artificial intelligence, allowing for classification to occur in either a supervised or unsupervised fashion (Meng et al., 2020). In contrast however, most of the recent work on data fusion and biosensor fusion is mostly concerned with simple accuracy – and little attention currently is focused on other explorations such as trend analysis. This is not without reason as healthcare applications generally champion accuracy, though there is still considerable space for data fusion to yield new insight into clinical practice as much right now is focused on informing the community of knowledge they already have. Regardless of the application, data fusion remains an exciting and active area of research providing near immediately translation of multiplexed systems into a real-world setting.

Since the first foundational work on biology and electronics by Glavani in the 18th century, bioelectronic research has expanded into an expansive field encompassing multidisciplinary approaches to developing the abio-bio interface. Guided by the biology, materials science, engineering, physics, and chemistry have intertwined to form a field where progress is made entirely at the interface. Biological components and materials have been shown to be extremely useful in integrating circuitry with biology by imparting biocompatibility, analyte specificity, electrical activity, or a combination of all three. Indeed, now it appears as if biological components are yet another part of a circuit diagram as evidenced by the variety of bioelectronic chemiresistors, capacitors, FETs, and more. However, despite this, the field still has expansive room to grow. Several nanomaterials only recently discovered now are being incorporated into bioelectronics (GQDs and CQDs), affording additional degrees of freedom due to integrating photonic activity into the electrical circuit. Furthermore, information fusion and machine learning serve to harness the power of real-time sensing and process data combinatorically to glean more information from the systems being sought for interrogation. With so much progress being made, new technologies and methodologies promise to ever increase the scope and impact of bioelectronics.

Prof. Dr. A. G.-E. is founder and scientific director of ABTECH Scientific, Inc., manufacturer of microfabricated biochip devices. He is a Fulbright Specialist sponsored by the US Department of State through World Learning and has been appointed to University of Tucumán, Argentina (UT - 2015) and Wroclaw University of Science and Technology, Poland (WUST – 2019). He has been funded by the Burroughs Wellcome Fund (2019). Support was received from the Consortium of the Center for Bioelectronics, Biosensors, and Biochips (C3B®) and ABTECH Scientific, Inc. The authors acknowledge the support of Texas Engineering Experiment Station (TEES) via a TEES Research Professorship.
